input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Digital Twin of a building (AECO domain) that integrates a static Building Information Model (BIM) with real‑time sensor data to create a cyber‑physical representation of the physical structure, its HVAC and electrical systems, and other critical building components. The twin supports the full life‑cycle of the building—from design and construction through operation and refurbishment—by providing continuous, real‑time insights, predictive analytics, and decision‑support based on the dynamic behavior of the physical asset.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The twin offers a portfolio of services: (1) monitoring of building performance through continuous ingestion of sensor, actuator, and control data; (2) real‑time control of HVAC, lighting, and other building systems via direct command interfaces; (3) predictive analytics and optimization of energy usage and system scheduling; (4) immersive visualization and interaction for Facility Managers via a 3‑D or mixed‑reality interface; and (5) extensible custom services and plugins provided by DT Engineers for domain‑specific analytics or third‑party integration. Each service is tailored to its target role—Building Control Engineers configure models and logic, Facility Managers monitor and influence operation, and DT Engineers extend functionality through custom plugins.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven engineering (MDE) framework that extracts structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generates deployable DT artifacts. BIM (Building Information Modeling) provides the semantic model of devices (sensors, actuators, controllers), while MDE principles enable transformation into runtime artifacts with minimal human effort. Runtime synchronization is achieved via real‑time data exchange protocols that keep the physical and virtual assets in lockstep. Visualization is delivered through an interactive 3‑D interface that can be extended via standardized plugin points. The modular architecture exposes extension points for custom services, analytics, and third‑party integration, thereby supporting continuous evolution of the twin’s capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real‑time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between the physical building and its digital twin, emphasizing minimal latency for real‑time insights and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a modular architecture pattern with a central DT Framework hub orchestrating component integration. Core elements include enriched BIM models, real‑time data ingestion modules, analytics services, and interactive visualization. Integration occurs through standardized extension points and bidirectional data exchange. Run‑time configuration management handles resource allocation and adaptability, while the hub coordinates system‑wide data flow, service evolution, and control actions.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is enabled via standard data exchange protocols and modular extension points that support interoperability across heterogeneous platforms. Specific protocols or dependency details are not provided, but the framework is designed to allow integration with enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, and custom services through these extensible interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework derives its Digital Twin from enriched Building Information Modelling (BIM) data, where geometric models of the physical building are augmented with semantic metadata that describes devices, sensors, actuators and control logic. This enriched BIM model is processed through a Model‑Driven Engineering pipeline that validates, converts, and configures the device data. The resulting structured representations are stored in Eclipse Ditto as twin state objects and published to a real‑time messaging layer using Eclipse Hono. The twin components are rendered in a high‑fidelity immersive visualisation built on Unreal Engine 5, where each BIM element is linked to a corresponding TwinComponent that emits and receives state updates. The overall data flow therefore consists of geometric data (BIM), device configuration metadata (e.g., ids, endpoint addresses, photometric values), real‑time sensor and command streams (Hono/Ditto), and visual representation (UE5).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the system is defined within the BIM environment by the Building Control Engineer and materialised as device models that are instantiated into the Digital Twin. The most concrete example is the LightTwinComponent, a light fixture whose state (on/off, brightness, colour‑temperature) is controlled by a UE 5 light element. Actuator commands are transmitted from the TwinComponent to the corresponding physical device via an DittoController, which publishes MQTT‑style messages to the physical PLC or driver exposed on a DittoConnection. Safety constraints and execution limits (e.g., maximum brightness, valid colour‑temperature range) are enforced by Ditto’s policy layer, which validates command payloads before delivery. Thus, remote control interfaces are provided through the Ditto connection endpoints, enabling real‑time manipulation of actuators from the immersive GUI or programmatic APIs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Physical sensing is captured by sensors that are defined as devices in the enriched BIM model. The Building Control Engineer specifies sensor types (e.g., occupancy, temperature, light intensity) and their spatial placement. Sensor data streams are transported in real‑time through Eclipse Hono to the Ditto twin state, where each reading is associated with its device id and validated against policy rules on unit consistency. The system supports continuous bidirectional integration, allowing the Digital Twin to retrieve the latest measurement values and visualise them within UE5. Specific sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or precision metrics are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through automated validation during the conversion of enriched BIM data. Eclipse Ditto’s policy infrastructure checks that incoming sensor values fall within predefined ranges and that actuator commands respect safety limits, thereby providing runtime verification of data integrity. Fault tolerance is achieved via Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery semantics and Ditto’s persistent event storage, which guarantees message durability across network disruptions. The combination of model‑driven validation, policy enforcement, and resilient messaging establishes a continuous quality‑assurance cycle for both models and the real‑time data that drive the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time continuous data exchange is achieved via Eclipse Hono for protocol‑agnostic transport (MQTT/AMQP/HTTP) and Eclipse Ditto as the digital twin service. Sensor telemetry (e.g., temperature, humidity, power consumption, occupancy, and device status) is streamed from physical assets to heterogenously published endpoints; each data item is wrapped as a JSON message with fields: tenantId, deviceId, timestamp, payload. Data is first validated against BIM‑derived schema rules (unit, range checks), then normalized (unit conversion), de‑duplicated, and enriched with metadata (endpoint addresses extracted via Ditto Configurator). Event triggers such as device registration, status change, or threshold breach cause immediate pushes; periodic heartbeats (every 30 s) ensure liveness. The flow proceeds to a dedicated Hono endpoint, brokers the message to Ditto’s Thing API, where it is stored and made available to the DT framework’s model‑integration service for incremental update of the BIM‑based digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flows are facilitated by the DT framework acting as a central hub; facility managers can issue commands via the DT interface to manage building controls in real‑time. The documents do not elaborate on specific command formats, communication protocols, authentication, or confirmation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports a complete digital twin lifecycle consisting of: (1) Device Modeling – Building Control Engineers populate enriched BIM models with explicit BCS component definitions (sensors, actuators, controllers) to overcome BIM’s static representation limitation; (2) Model‑to‑Artifact Transformation – Using Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles the enriched BIM is parsed to a structured semantic representation and automatically compiled into deployable digital twin artifacts; (3) Deployment – The generated artifacts are deployed to the runtime environment with minimal manual configuration, giving the physical building a digital counterpart; (4) Runtime Operation – Continuous bi‑directional, real‑time data exchange synchronizes physical asset states with the twin, while interactive visualization dashboards allow Facility Managers to monitor and control operations; (5) Service Extension – Through the framework’s modular architecture with standardized extension points, custom DT services and analytics can be added, evolving the twin in line with operational demands. Lifecycle governance is achieved via the standardized extension point architecture, ensuring traceability from BIM definition to runtime services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Twinning is engineered by first authoring device and control logic directly within the BIM environment. Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators and controllers, embedding BCS level details. The pipeline then applies MDE to translate the enriched BIM into structured, semantically‑rich artifact models, which are automatically compiled into deployable digital twin components. Deployment automation produces runtime‑ready artifacts that establish bidirectional, real‑time connections with the physical infrastructure. The framework’s modular architecture exposes standardized extension points that allow DT Engineers to integrate custom analytics, AI‑driven services or other domain‑specific plugins. Version control, quality assurance, testing and validation procedures are not mentioned in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system offers interactive visualization dashboards that provide immersive interfaces for real‑time monitoring of the digital twin. Facility Managers utilize these dashboards to observe system states and issue control commands directly to the physical building, thus enabling immediate operational decision making. While no explicit analytics, confidence metrics or decision support algorithms are described, the real‑time data exchange layer delivers on‑premises data streams that could be leveraged for future decision‑support enhancements.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The documentation does not specify adherence to particular industry standards, certification requirements or interoperability frameworks. The framework does, however, employ a modular architecture with standardized extension points to integrate third‑party services and analytics, but no explicit compliance with AECO‑specific or other standardised schemas (e.g., IFC, BIM/IFC) is mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the Digital Twin as a cyber-physical representation in the AECO domain, correctly noting the integration of static BIM models with real-time sensor data and full life-cycle support. However, it lists 'HVAC and electrical systems' as specific components, whereas the provided source text only explicitly mentions 'lighting systems' in the context of its case studies and generally refers to 'physical assets' or 'infrastructure'. Therefore, the extraction relies on outside knowledge for these specific system types.

The extraction provides a comprehensive list of services—monitoring, control, predictive analytics, visualization, and extensibility—and correctly maps them to the specific user roles defined in the source (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer). It accurately captures the capabilities like 3D visualization and plugin extensions. However, it specifies 'control of HVAC' in addition to lighting, which is not supported by the source text (which focuses on lighting in the examples), making it slightly speculative regarding specific controlled subsystems.

The extraction correctly identifies the Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) framework, the role of BIM as a semantic model for devices, and the automatic generation of runtime artifacts. It accurately describes the modular architecture and extension points. However, it generalizes the specific tools mentioned in the source, referring to 'real-time data exchange protocols' instead of naming 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' and 'interactive 3-D interface' instead of 'Unreal Engine', thus missing some technical detail present in the source.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The source text describes a model-driven framework for deploying a Digital Twin for a building, focusing on the internal roles (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) and the architecture of a single twin instance. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination hierarchies between different twins.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed summary of the system's architecture and orchestration. However, it includes the phrase 'handles resource allocation' regarding run-time configuration, which is not supported by the text (the text mentions 'adjustments' and 'adaptability' but not resource allocation). Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange' and 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of standardized extension points and the goal of interoperability with heterogeneous platforms. It accurately notes that specific protocols (beyond the tools Hono/Ditto) and dependency details are not explicitly listed in the text. The claim about integration with 'other Digital Twins' is a reasonable inference from the goal of 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' but not explicitly detailed. Supported by quotes: 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms' and 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems'.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a

steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

The description accurately synthesizes the engineering process described in Section III-A, including the roles of Building Control Engineers and DT Engineers, and the use of MDE for transformation. The extraction correctly identifies the system's capabilities as focused on interactive visualization, real-time monitoring, and direct control commands. It accurately notes the absence of specific analytics or decision support algorithms in the provided text, distinguishing between the framework's current features and potential future enhancements.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
